paragraphs.body: Media depicts conspiracy theorist believers to be simple-minded, unhinged or mostly mentally ill who go on rampages about some abstract theory like the government put cameras in birds or the government is slowly killing all their citizens.

But there are a multitude of reasons why people, ordinary people, are prone to believing in conspiracy theories and it is fascinating to wonder what factor specifically contributes to that? Shauna Bowes and other researchers hypothesized that it was a combination of personality traits and motivations associated with conspiratorial thinking.

Shauna Bowes is a doctoral student in clinical psychology, that did research behind conspiracy theories. She published a paper, “The Conspiratorial Mind: A Meta-Analytic Review of Motivational and Personological Correlates” where she goes into more detail on her observations from her study and the connections found in the psychology behind believing in conspiracy theories.

annotations.comment: Don't forget the summary templates and summary moves that we learned about! They're on Canvas.
annotations.context_left: from her study and the connections found in the psychology behind believing in conspiracy theories.

paragraphs.body: The conversation is about why some people are willing to believe in conspiracy theories.

It is understood that people turn to conspiracy theories to fulfill their deprived motivational needs or to make sense of something distressing or confusing. In Bowes research, she also claims that some people turn towards believing conspiracy theories for three other reasons. One, overall being that people need to feel safe in their environments and a need for understanding. Another being, they want to feel like the “community they identify with is superior to others.” Finally, evidence showed that many people who believe in conspiracy theories were “motivated by social relationships.”
To summarize the third reasoning, social relationships relate to the surrounding environment of the person. That is a huge factor in what theories someone believes and which ones they do not.

But what is more fascinating is that personality types also contribute to those decisions. In the results of the study, Bowes concluded that personalities with a sense of “antagonism toward others and high levels of paranoia” were more prone to believing in conspiracy theories.

Bowes claimed that those who strongly believed conspiracy theories were “more likely to be insecure, paranoid, emotionally volatile, impulsive, suspicious, withdrawn, manipulative, egocentric and eccentric”

In my view, I agree that certain personality types are susceptible to believing in more conspiracy theories, that some people just want to feel superior or unique by believing in a theory and that the theories you believe in are influenced by the environment in which you grew up or are currently in.

Your environment and the social relationships you have shape your reality and alter your perception of the world. Thats why everyone believes and sees things differently, so with that in mind, I agree that social relationships are the overall major contributing factor to why people in conspiracy theories.

Conspiracy theories are something I enjoy, not because I believe in any crazy ones, but I find it entertaining to see what other people believe in. In my experience, whenever I see an online community tab or comment section discussing a theory, I find absurd or interesting, I find it more fascinating that the people who believe in them create such a safe space that the conversations are normalized. It is as if they all collectively brainwashed and are discussing the crazy theory into reality or at least their realities. But if you took those crazy conversations outside of the community it would sound unbelievable and insane to others.

It gets me thinking about how you could take that type of thinking and make a connection to cults, religions, everyday life, accepted societal norms, etc. That is where my obsession on this topic lies, the different connections that can be made and the amusing and mostly funny conspiracy theories online.
I find it also to be just fun to read!

Why is it fascinating to me that our brains are powerful enough to alter our memories? Change our reality. Make us question what was real and what we made up. How our brain can block out the pain from a traumatizing event so we can then live happier lives. Our own brains want us to be happy. And yet, most humans have such pessimistic views of the world. Why is that?

annotations.comment: You're clearly brainstorming, here, and thinking through your own ideas--but remember that we're writing a cohesive essay. What is your central focusing idea? Who is your audience? What is your essay's purpose? What are the " big ideas" that you need to develop to support, organize, and develop this essay?
annotations.context_left: ns want us to be happy. And yet, most humans have such pessimistic views of the world. Why is that?

paragraphs.body: I do not know why I wonder about these things or find them so interesting. I do not know if I even believe in the thought that you could speak things into existence. Like how people say positive affirmations or those manifestation journals. If you asked me a few years ago if those practices worked, I would have looked at you funny. That just goes to show how pessimistic our society is, how normal it is. But now, since the start of my fascination with conspiracy theories, I realized its more interesting to me the mindset of someone who believes these theories. But that goes further, it goes to the human brain itself and the power behind language. The power behind speaking things into your own reality.

annotations.text: If you asked me a few years ago if those practices worked, I would have looked at you funny. That just goes to show how pessimistic our society
annotations.comment: This is a moment of friction. Let's talk :)

paragraphs.body: People's perceptions are their reality.

annotations.comment: Yes. Interesting. This relates to your ideas in the paragraph above. Clearly, then, this idea is meaningful for this essay! Let's talk, then, about the audience/purpose and--ultimately--the "map" of this essay.

paragraphs.body: We do this every day. When people wash their face and use their serums they believe those products are making a difference, that those products and serums are removing their wrinkles or brightening the circles under their eyes but do those products really do that? Are there actual differences? Or is the product only working in your mind because you believe it is actually doing something?
I wonder that. Is it really that easy to convince yourself? To change your perception on things-- yes, it is.
It feels nice to believe that you are right. That things look, feel, or just are better.

annotations.comment: Is this idea important in terms of your audience? Are there people who just want to be "right"? This is a slightly different idea than the ones previous, but you might be "writing your way" into this essay. We need to figure out what this essay's _really_ about. :)

paragraphs.body: It’s the same thoughts behind people believing in conspiracy theories or positive affirmations. It's the comfort and ease on your mind I love so much.

In my personal experience, I believe I have altered how I recall some traumatizing memories. I have altered them to a point where I feel comfortable whenever I get reminded and acknowledging that whatever happened hurt. Its like the saying “Time heals all”.

annotations.text: I believe I have altered how I recall some traumatizing memories. I have altered them to a point where I feel comfortable whenever I get reminded and acknowledging that whatever happened hurt. Its like the saying “Time heals all”.
annotations.comment: Interesting moment. This could relate to the idea of "perception" and reality.

paragraphs.body: Except, time does not do anything at all. It is you reducing the pain from the reality of what really happened. Or so I believe.

annotations.text: time does not do anything at all.

paragraphs.body: How can time heal when we created time to build structure and to measure our lives? It can’t. That is not what it was made for.

annotations.text: How can time heal when we created time to build structure and to measure our lives? It can’t.
annotations.comment: These ideas about time seem to be new and different. I'm not quite sure how they relate to an essay with one clear central focusing idea? Let's discuss.

paragraphs.body: So, if we can repeatedly tell ourselves things in our head that can alter our memories and change our perception of life. Then wouldn’t it be true that if we said those same statements aloud, that our reality would follow and change around us? Thats the question I raise. If we all believed this, then we would start prioritizing saying more positive thoughts aloud.

annotations.text: If we all believed this, then we would start prioritizing saying more positive thoughts aloud.
annotations.comment: Okay. Yes. Interesting. You have many compelling ideas in this draft; let's find your audience/purpose/center of gravity and create a map!

paragraphs.body: The power behind saying statements aloud is strong enough to change our reality. It just depends on what statements you make. Like saying negative things might have a real affect on how you brain acts, same with if you said positive statements.
That is so crazy to me.
That is when I started looking at conspiracy theories. I wanted to know if there was a way to reverse your brain. A way to make your brain go back to the way it used to be before it got triggered.
All I found was scientific theories that traumatic events can trigger something like schizophrenia or paranoia, but no research has found that a brain can go back.

annotations.comment: is this essay about trauma or, rather, an overall optimistic outlook on life? The audience would vary, I think, depending on your answer. Let's talk.

paragraphs.body: I also found all those wacky conspiracy theories that kept me entertained. Distracted.

Now the reason I started believing that you could speak things into your reality is because deep down that is the only solution, I have left to believe in that could “reverse” whatever has happened to my brain.
Now that sounds crazy.

I also wondered if a brain could be reversed back to how it originally was in positive affirmation because I wished nothing more than for my grandma to return to the way she was before her Alzheimer's Disease got worse.

annotations.text: I wished nothing more than for my grandma to return to the way she was before her Alzheimer's Disease got worse.
annotations.comment: This is such a heartfelt and authentic moment to reveal. Thank you for trusting me with it. I wonder, though, if it's another different idea? We'll discuss :)

paragraphs.body: As a kid, during the years I can barely remember and have mostly forgotten, my grandmother’s mind was at its best. Do I deep down regret not being able to remember the details? I guess so since I so desperately wanted simple phrases to change both of our realities. Now that my grandmother has passed, I only say simple, positive phrases to myself. For my own reality. I still want so badly for positive phrases to change our realities. I want to know if the power of language and speech really is as strong as some people could have you believe. Is it so strong that it can alter the way you think and the way you perceive the environment you're in? Tons of unanswered questions that fascinate me.

annotations.text: I still want so badly for positive phrases to change our realities.
annotations.comment: This friction is powerful. There's so much for us to talk about :)
